system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — complete-chord-melody

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc

O'Rourke's Complete Chord Melody is organized as a top-down, recipe-first method that begins with a working arrangement procedure on page one and only later supplies the theory and vocabulary needed to execute it. Chapter 1 (Module 1) opens with the four-step chord-melody method - pick a suitable tune, transpose it into a workable key, learn the melody on the top 2-3 strings, then add chord voicings below the melody notes - which functions as the book's operating system; every later chapter feeds inputs into one of those four steps. Chapter 2 (Module 2) builds the chord vocabulary the method indexes against, organized around the Three Harmonic Families taxonomy (Major / Minor / Dominant) and the chord collection construct that makes the dictionary lookup-able by melody note. Chapters 3-4 (Module 3) execute an end-to-end arrangement workflow, splitting it into a front half of melody-fingering, comping, and harmonic analysis (Chapter 3) and a back half that formalizes the load-bearing 'painting by numbers' rule and its remediation strategies (Chapter 4). Chapter 5 (Module 4) transitions from trio chord-melody to solo guitar by adding bass responsibility, shell voicings, and four core devices (arpeggiation, octaves, diads, walking bass). Chapter 6 closes Module 4 with annotated example arrangements as study material. Chapter 7 (Module 5) is the advanced reharmonization core, codifying five named substitution rules. Chapters 8-9 are appendices: the chord dictionary that backs the painting-by-numbers lookup, and a fingerstyle-technique appendix justifying the right-hand method underwriting the whole enterprise.

## The Four-Step Method as Central Thesis

The entire book is an elaboration of Chapter 1's four-step recipe, and almost every later rule is best read as a constraint, lookup table, or escape hatch attached to one of those four steps. Step 1 (tune selection) is constrained by the ballad-not-bebop rule: slower tempos with longer-held melody notes are practically easier for beginners. Step 2 (transposition) is constrained by the range rule of thumb - find a key where the melody sits on the top 3 strings between roughly the 3rd and 9th frets - explicitly defended against two failure modes (too high sounds thin, too low sounds dark and muddy). Step 3 (melody on the top strings) imposes a horizontal rather than positional fretting approach and is reaffirmed in Chapter 3 with an explicit carve-out that occasional dips to the 3rd or 4th string are acceptable. Step 4 (voicing the chords below the melody) is governed by the headline pedagogical rule that you don't need a chord on every melody note: chords go on long-held notes and at chord changes, exploiting the guitar's quick decay so that a chord change implies sustain rather than holding the note out. The four-step recipe is therefore not a beginner's onramp that the book later abandons - it is the load-bearing thesis the entire 250-plus pages of vocabulary, analysis, dictionary, and substitution machinery serves.

## The Painting-by-Numbers Lookup System

Chapters 3-4 supply the operational core that makes Step 4 of the four-step method mechanical rather than improvised. The linchpin construct is interval analysis: knowing each melody note's interval relative to its underlying chord is what enables dictionary lookup of a voicing with that interval on top. O'Rourke flags the most common beginner mistake - labeling intervals against the overall key rather than the current chord - and elevates this to his explicit 'golden rule of interval analysis,' backed by the major-scale-of-the-current-chord's-root reference rule. The closed chord-tone vocabulary used as dictionary index keys is fixed: 1, b9, 9, b3, 3, 11, #11, 5, b13, 13, b7, 7, plus PT (passing tone) as the escape hatch for unharmonized chromatic notes and an edge-case label for melody notes that anticipate the next chord. Dictionary lookup itself is keyed on three inputs - the melody string, the chord type at that moment, and the melody note's interval relative to that chord - and a dictionary voicing is transposed into the tune's key by sliding the shape until its top note lands on the melody fret. The painting-by-numbers rule itself is stated as: pick voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note relative to the underlying chord. The whole system is what makes the four-step method teachable rather than merely demonstrable.

## The Three Harmonic Families and the Chord Collection

Chapter 2's organizing taxonomy is that every chord belongs to one of three harmonic families - Major, Minor, or Dominant - with minor refined into m7 and m7b5 sub-families and dominant refined into natural ('7') and altered ('7alt') sub-families, where the sub-family distinction drives major-vs-minor ii-V-i voicing choice. The central indexing unit is the chord collection, defined as a group of chords covering every diatonic interval as the top note, which is what makes the dictionary indexable by melody note. The dictionary is structured around ii-V-I precisely because that single progression covers all three harmonic families, and collections are duplicated into 1st-string and 2nd-string variants to match the top-2-string melody constraint from Module 1. On the theory side, the chapter defines a chord symbol as 'an attempt to describe a sound,' establishes stacking thirds as the core construction principle, and explains 9/11/13 extensions as continuing to stack thirds past the 7th. The load-bearing pedagogical rule of Chapter 2 is the gap between the theoretically correct chord and the playable guitar voicing, filled by an explicit omittable-notes list: 5th, root, 11th, and 9th first, with 3rd and 7th sometimes omittable in complex chords. A final terminological rule - surrounding chords define a chord's name as much as its notes do - foreshadows the substitution machinery of Chapter 7.

## Restraint as Aesthetic Axiom

A single aesthetic commitment surfaces in every chapter and is the book's most consistent prescriptive voice: less is more. Chapter 1 states it as 'don't harmonize every note.' Chapter 4 restates it as 'less is more' (chords placed selectively are easier on the hands and usually imply the harmony adequately) and as 'playability over ambition' (easier voicings flow musically better). Chapter 4's three remediation strategies for awkward passages all express the same axiom: cut notes out of the chord, substitute a different harmonization device such as parallel octaves or diads, or remove the difficult voicing entirely - which O'Rourke explicitly identifies as his own most-used escape valve. Chapter 6 sanctions rhythmic adjustment of the melody to facilitate fretboard jumps, framing the arranger's pragmatics as a rule rather than a compromise. Chapter 7's substitution machinery closes with the explicit restraint rule that subtlety beats density: keep things simple and use substitutions occasionally for color. Color itself is localized: Chapter 4 locates the 'jazzy' sound specifically in extensions and tensions added to dominant chords as the targeted chord type for color, which is consistent with Chapter 8's dictionary structure (a dedicated section for altered/extended dominants as the method's color resource).

## The Solo-Guitar Extension and Its Devices

Chapter 5 extends the four-step method to solo guitar by adding bass responsibility to Step 4. The aesthetic strategy is alternation rather than simultaneity - walking bass, then chord line, then unaccompanied single line - because the listener's mind fills in the gaps to produce 'the illusion of solo guitar.' The load-bearing difference from trio playing is greater prominence of bass notes on the 5th and 6th strings since there is no bass player, and a specific key-selection rule follows: prefer A, E, or D so open strings can carry bass notes and free the fretting hand. The diagnostic for thin-sounding arrangements is bass voiced too close to the upper harmony. The solo-guitar voicing primitive is the shell voicing - root, 3rd, and 7th only - derived from bar chords by dropping the 5th first as the rule for which note to cut, with the 6th available as a substitute for the 7th for a warmer, more resolved color, and a general refinement rule to avoid doubled notes unless the doubling happens to sound good. The four core solo-guitar devices are defined explicitly: arpeggiation (plucking chord notes one at a time, used as a melody-space filler in slow ballads), octaves (Wes-Montgomery-style melody doubling one octave down, supported by the muting technique of resting the first finger gently on the unwanted in-between string), diads (Barney-Kessel-style parallel 3rds or 6ths as a primary melody-support layer), and walking bass (a four-step build filling beats 2 and 3 with diatonic, chromatic, or arpeggio notes between the beat-1 root and the beat-4 approach note). A chromatic approach-chord rule completes the toolkit: repeat the same voicing one fret away from the target. An intermediate drill - adding a bass note under the melody at each chord change - builds the ear before voicings get added.

## The Five Substitution Rules

Chapter 7 is the substitution-and-reharmonization core, opening with the foundational definition that a substitution simply means replacing one chord with another and then codifying five distinct named substitution rules. (1) The Tritone Substitution Rule: any chord can be replaced by one whose root is a tritone away, justified theoretically by the shared 3rd/7th guide tones that swap places between the two chords (the common-tones-enable-substitution principle that grounds the rest of the chapter). (2) The I-to-vi substitution rule and (3) the I-to-iii substitution rule, both grounded in relative-key thinking and the umbrella concept of chord addition. (4) The Minor for Dominant Substitution Rule, borrowed from Pat Martino's minor conversion concept and operationalized as substituting the iim7 from the dominant's 5th. (5) The back-cycling substitution rule, framed as movement through the circle of fourths - the most common root movement in Western music - and the melodic common-tone substitution rule that drives modern Rosenwinkel/Kreisberg-style colors. The chapter's characteristic pedagogical move pairs these rules with two concrete practice prescriptions: the metronome on beats 2 and 4 as the jazz-preferred form, and the load-bearing per-session quantum of just one or two bars worked slowly.

## The Fingerstyle Foundation

Chapter 9 (Appendix 2) is not peripheral - it justifies the right-hand method underwriting the entire enterprise with the claim that fingerstyle is what enables multiple simultaneous voices and therefore the whole chord-melody enterprise. It establishes the PIMAC finger-naming convention (p/i/m/a/c) used throughout the picking-pattern markings and devotes early space to ergonomic posture rules. The two canonical strokes are defined as the foundational pluck vocabulary - rest stroke (finger lands on the next string) and free stroke (finger flies free) - with the explicit preference rule that free stroke is the main technique for jazz guitar. O'Rourke's characteristic moves are tight mechanical specifications: thumb motion comes from the lowest joint with a small rowing-paddle circular motion reserved for bass notes and basslines; multi-string plucking requires the wrist rolled toward the headstock to keep thumb and index from obstructing each other. Load-bearing combinatorial rules close the chapter: the canonical two-finger pairs are p+i or i+m, the canonical three-finger groupings are pim or ima, voicings cap at four notes and the pinky is omitted from the picking hand, and plucked notes must sound simultaneously to register as a chord rather than an arpeggiation. Chapter 1 had already foreshadowed this with its plectrum-bass-then-strum technique for players using a pick - the right-hand technique choice is presented as one of the method's load-bearing inputs from the start.

## The Method as a Unit

Taken together, the book teaches an integrated method with five interlocking commitments. First, the four-step recipe (pick tune, transpose for range, melody on top strings, voicings below) is the book's central thesis and every other chapter is a constraint, lookup table, or escape hatch on one of those four steps. Second, the Three Harmonic Families taxonomy plus the chord collection construct makes the chord vocabulary indexable by melody note, which is what makes Step 4 mechanical rather than improvised. Third, the painting-by-numbers rule - pick voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note relative to the underlying chord - executes the lookup, governed by the golden-rule-of-interval-analysis (interval relates to the current chord, not the key) and a fixed closed vocabulary of chord-tone labels. Fourth, restraint is the aesthetic axiom that runs through every chapter: don't harmonize every note, less is more, playability over ambition, removing a voicing entirely is a sanctioned move, and substitutions are for occasional color rather than density. Fifth, solo-guitar arranging extends the trio method by adding bass responsibility on the 5th/6th strings, shell voicings as the primitive, and four named devices (arpeggiation, octaves, diads, walking bass), while Chapter 7's five named substitution rules supply the reharmonization toolkit and Chapter 9's fingerstyle technique supplies the right-hand foundation that makes simultaneous voices physically possible. The book's pedagogical voice throughout is procedural and permissive: rules are stated, then explicit carve-outs and escape valves are sanctioned (cut notes, substitute diads/octaves, remove the chord, rewrite the melody's rhythm), so that the four-step recipe remains executable even when its strict output isn't playable.

# Chapter 1 — Module 1 - Chord Melody Quickstart

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 lays out O'Rourke's foundational four-step chord-melody recipe that the entire course operationalizes: pick a suitable tune, transpose it if needed for range, learn the melody on the top 2 or 3 strings, then choose chord voicings below the melody notes (Four-step chord melody method). For step 1 he restricts beginners to ballads rather than bebop because slower tempos and longer-held melody notes are practically easier (Tune selection rule), and for step 2 he gives a concrete fretboard-region heuristic — find a key where the melody sits on the top 3 strings between roughly the 3rd and 9th frets (Range rule of thumb) — naming the two failure modes the rule is designed to avoid: too high sounds thin, too low sounds dark and muddy (Range failure modes). The top-string canvas forces a horizontal rather than positional fretting approach (Horizontal melody fingering). The chapter's headline pedagogical rule is that you don't need a chord on every melody note (Don't harmonize every note), operationalized by placing chords on long-held notes (Place chords on long notes) and exploiting the guitar's quick decay by using a chord change to imply sustain rather than holding the note out (Sustain workaround). Load-bearing terminology introduced includes the four-step method itself, the top-2-strings constraint, and right-hand technique choice (Right-hand technique choice) — with a concrete plectrum move of plucking the bass note first then quickly strumming the rest of the chord to negotiate tricky string skips (Plectrum bass-then-strum).

# Chapter 2 — Module 2 - Chord Vocabulary

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 builds the chord vocabulary that the rest of the method indexes against, beginning with O'Rourke's three-family taxonomy — every chord belongs to Major, Minor, or Dominant (Three Harmonic Families) — then refining minor into m7 and m7b5 sub-families and dominant into natural ('7') and altered ('7alt') sub-families that drive major-vs-minor ii-V-i voicing choice (Minor and dominant sub-families). The central organizing unit is the chord collection, defined as a group of chords covering every diatonic interval as the top note so the dictionary becomes indexable by melody note (Chord collection definition), and the dictionary is structured around ii-V-I because that single progression covers all three harmonic families (Why ii V I for collections). Collections are duplicated into 1st-string and 2nd-string variants to match the top-2-string melody constraint from Module 1 (2nd-string collection rationale). On the theory side the chapter defines a chord symbol as an attempt to describe a sound (Chord symbol definition), establishes stacking thirds as the core construction principle (Stacking thirds), and explains 9/11/13 extensions as continuing to stack thirds past the 7th (Second-octave structures). The load-bearing pedagogical rule is the gap between the theoretically correct chord and the playable guitar voicing (Theoretical vs playable), filled by an explicit omittable-notes list — 5th, root, 11th, and 9th, with 3rd and 7th sometimes omittable in complex chords (Omittable notes list). Final terminology: the diatonic-vs-altered distinction that drives chord-symbol notation (Extensions vs altered tensions), and the rule that surrounding chords define a chord's name as much as its notes do (Context defines chord name).

# Chapter 3 — Module 3 - Let's Create a Chord Melody Arrangement (First Steps & Harmony Analysis)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 walks the student through the front half of an actual arrangement workflow — melody fingering, comping, and harmonic analysis — before any voicing selection happens. It reaffirms the top-2-string canvas with an explicit carve-out that occasional dips to the 3rd or 4th string are fine (Top-2-string melody constraint), insists the arrangement be notated on paper or software so it can be iteratively refined (Write down the arrangement), and prescribes a pre-step of simply comping the song's basic chords in a band context to train the ear before voicing choices begin (Basic comping before voicing). The harmonic analysis pass has three explicit outputs: chord qualities (major/minor/dominant), key changes, and recognizable progressions like ii-V-I (Harmonic analysis goals), notated using uppercase Roman numerals for major/dominant and lowercase for minor (Roman numeral conventions). The linchpin of the whole 'painting by numbers' method is then introduced: knowing each melody note's interval relative to its underlying chord is what enables dictionary lookup of a voicing with that interval on top (Why interval analysis). O'Rourke flags the most common beginner mistake — labeling intervals against the overall key rather than the current chord (Interval relates to current chord) — and reinforces this as his headline 'golden rule' (Golden rule of interval analysis), backed by the rule that the reference is always the major scale of the current chord's root (Major scale reference rule). Load-bearing terminology includes the closed chord-tone vocabulary used as dictionary index keys — 1, b9, 9, b3, 3, 11, #11, 5, b13, 13, b7, 7 (Chord-tone vocabulary) — plus the PT (passing tone) escape hatch for unharmonized chromatic notes (Passing-tone label) and the edge case where a melody note anticipating the next chord is labeled against that next chord (Melody anticipates next chord).

# Chapter 4 — Module 3 - Painting by Numbers and Arrangement Examples

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 completes the 'painting by numbers' arranging method and addresses what to do when the rule's strict output isn't playable. Chords get placed on the first melody note of each bar and at chord changes (Where to place chords), with the single load-bearing rule being to pick voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note relative to the underlying chord (Painting by numbers rule), softened by a carve-out that a voicing can be slightly adapted as long as the melody note ends up on top (Adapt voicing for melody). Dictionary lookup is keyed on three inputs: the melody string, the chord type at that moment, and the melody note's interval relative to that chord (Three voicing-lookup inputs), and a dictionary voicing is transposed into the tune's key by sliding the shape until its top note lands on the melody fret (Transpose by sliding shape). The chapter's pedagogical center of gravity is restraint: less is more, since chords placed selectively are easier on the hands and usually imply the harmony adequately (Less is more), and playability beats ambition because easier voicings flow musically better (Playability over ambition). O'Rourke gives three remediation strategies for awkward passages — cut notes out of the chord (Three options for hard passages), substitute a different harmonization device such as parallel octaves or diads (Diads/octaves alternative), or remove the difficult voicing entirely, which he calls out as his own most-used escape valve (Removing chord entirely). The chapter closes by locating the 'jazzy' sound specifically in extensions and tensions added to dominant chords as the targeted chord type for color (Tension from dominants).

# Chapter 5 — Module 4 - Arranging For Solo Guitar (Techniques)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 transitions from trio chord-melody to solo guitar arranging, organized around an aesthetic strategy and four core devices. The aesthetic strategy is alternation rather than simultaneity — walking bass, then chord line, then unaccompanied single line — because the listener's mind fills in the gaps to produce the illusion of a full band (Illusion of solo guitar). The load-bearing difference from trio playing is greater prominence of bass notes on the 5th and 6th strings since there is no bass player to cover that role (Bass on 5th/6th strings), and a specific key-selection rule follows: prefer A, E, or D so open strings can carry bass notes and free the fretting hand (Open-string keys). The diagnostic for thin-sounding arrangements is bass voiced too close to the upper harmony (Bass-upper proximity issue). The solo-guitar voicing primitive is the shell voicing — root, 3rd, and 7th only (Shell voicing definition) — derived from bar chords by dropping the 5th first as the rule for which note to cut (Drop the 5th), with the 6th available as a substitute for the 7th for a warmer, more resolved color (6 or 7 substitution), and a general refinement rule to avoid doubled notes unless the doubling happens to sound good (Avoid doubled notes). Workflow-wise, an intermediate drill of adding a bass note under the melody at each chord change builds the ear before voicings get added (Bass-notes-at-changes drill). The chapter then defines the four core solo-guitar devices: arpeggiation as plucking chord notes one at a time (Arpeggiation definition), octaves as Wes-Montgomery-style melody doubling one octave down (Octaves definition) supported by the muting technique of resting the first finger gently on the unwanted in-between string (Octave muting technique), and diads as Barney-Kessel-style parallel 3rds or 6ths (Diads definition). It closes with a four-step recipe for walking bass — fill beats 2 and 3 with diatonic, chromatic, or arpeggio notes between the beat-1 root and the beat-4 approach note (Walking bass four-step build) — and a chromatic approach-chord rule of repeating the same voicing one fret away from the target (Chromatic approach chord).

# Chapter 6 — Module 4 - Solo Guitar Arrangement Examples

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 opens Module 4 by framing complete solo-guitar arrangements as study material, with O'Rourke's core pedagogical instruction that students should physically learn the arrangements rather than just read them, because hands-on internalization is how the underlying techniques transfer to a student's own work (Learn the arrangements hands-on). The chapter then walks through the load-bearing arranging devices visible across the example renditions: arpeggiation as a melody-space filler in slow ballads (Arpeggiation fills ballad space), diads as a primary melody-support layer (Diads support the melody), and the up-tempo stack of diads, octaves, and passing chords used together to drive faster arrangements (Up-tempo devices stack). O'Rourke's characteristic move surfaces in the explicit permission to rewrite melodic rhythm when fingering demands it: rhythmic adjustment of the melody is acceptable to facilitate fretboard jumps (Manipulate melody rhythm for fingering), framing the arranger's pragmatics as a sanctioned rule rather than a compromise.

# Chapter 7 — Module 5 - More Advanced Arranging Techniques

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 is the substitution-and-reharmonization core of the method, opening with the foundational definition that a substitution simply means replacing one chord with another (Substitution defined) and then codifying five distinct substitution approaches as explicit rules. Tritone substitution is introduced as the most useful jazz substitution (Tritone substitution defined), justified theoretically by the shared 3rd/7th guide tones that swap places between the two chords (Guide tones explain tritone sub, Common-tones enable substitution), and formalized as the canonical Tritone Substitution Rule that any chord can be replaced by one whose root is a tritone away (Tritone Substitution Rule). Relative-key thinking grounds the next pair: the I-to-vi rule and the I-to-iii rule that license major-to-minor reharmonization via shared key signature (Relative key definition, I to vi substitution rule, I to iii substitution rule), framed under the umbrella concept of chord addition (Chord addition defined). O'Rourke then borrows Pat Martino's minor conversion concept to license minor-for-anything substitution (Minor conversion concept) and codifies a concrete Minor for Dominant Substitution Rule using the iim7 from the dominant's 5th (Minor for Dominant Substitution Rule), followed by back cycling as movement through the circle of fourths—the most common root movement in Western music (Back cycling root movement, Back cycling substitution rule)—and finally the melodic common tone substitution rule that drives modern Rosenwinkel/Kreisberg-style colors (Melodic common tone substitution rule). The master's characteristic pedagogical move is the explicit restraint rule that subtlety beats density: keep things simple and use substitutions occasionally for color (Don't overuse substitutions), paired with two concrete practice prescriptions—the metronome on beats 2 and 4 as the jazz-preferred form (Practice with metronome on 2 and 4) and the load-bearing per-session quantum of just one or two bars worked slowly (Practice 1-2 bars per session).

# Chapter 8 — Appendix 1 - Chord Dictionaries

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Chapter 8 is a reference appendix rather than instructional material, consolidating the chord voicings introduced earlier in the method into a single chord-melody dictionary keyed to Module 3 (Chord melody dictionary purpose). The load-bearing organizing taxonomy is a four-way split of ii V i voicings by quality and string set: major ii V I on string set 4321 and on string set 5432 (Major II V I string set 4321, Major II V I string set 5432), and minor ii V i on the same two string sets with the b5 and b9 alterations characteristic of the minor cadence (Minor II V I string set 4321, Minor II V I string set 5432). A second section catalogues altered/extended dominants as the method's color resource (Tensions and extensions on dominants) and then pivots to the solo-guitar voicing reference for Module 4 (Solo guitar shell voicings), built on the structural backbone of 6th-string-root shell voicings (Shell voicings root on 6th) plus a catalog of extensions stacked on top of those shells (Shell voicing extensions catalog)—the load-bearing harmonic vocabulary O'Rourke uses for solo arranging.

# Chapter 9 — Appendix 2 - Fingerstyle Training

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 is a fingerstyle-technique appendix that justifies the right-hand method underwriting the entire book, opening with the claim that fingerstyle is what enables multiple simultaneous voices and therefore the whole chord-melody enterprise (Fingerstyle plays multiple voices). It establishes the PIMAC finger-naming convention (p/i/m/a/c) used throughout the picking-pattern markings (PIMAC finger naming) and devotes early space to ergonomics: the foundational rule that hand position must promote agility from day one (Early hand position matters) and a four-rule posture catalog covering wrist curve, wrist-arm alignment, relaxation, and optional fingernails (Tips for Correct Fingerstyle Hand Placement). It then defines the two canonical strokes—rest stroke (finger lands on the next string) and free stroke (finger flies free)—as the foundational pluck vocabulary (Rest stroke vs free stroke defined), with the explicit preference rule that free stroke is the main technique for jazz guitar (Prefer free stroke for jazz). O'Rourke's characteristic moves are tight mechanical specifications: thumb motion comes from the lowest joint with a small rowing-paddle circular motion (Thumb motion mechanics) reserved for bass notes and basslines (Thumb role in fingerstyle jazz), and multi-string plucking requires the wrist rolled toward the headstock to keep thumb and index from obstructing each other (Wrist angle for multi-string plucks). Load-bearing rules close the chapter: the canonical two-finger pairs are p+i or i+m (Two-finger pluck combinations), the canonical three-finger groupings are pim or ima (Three-note pluck combinations), voicings cap at four notes and the pinky is omitted from the picking hand (Four-finger maximum, skip pinky), and plucked notes must sound simultaneously to register as a chord rather than an arpeggiation (Sound multi-string notes simultaneously).


COMMITTED CHAPTER FILES (verbatim quotes + rationales):

# Chapter 1 — Module 1 - Chord Melody Quickstart

_Pages 15-31._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Four-step chord melody method

> Pick a suitable tune
>    Change the key if necessary for better range and playability
>    Learn the melody on top 2 (or 3) strings
>    Choose chord voicings to add below melody notes

**Significance (p. 17).** This is O'Rourke's foundational four-step recipe that the entire course operationalizes.

## Tune selection rule

> For your first arrangement, I recommend you stick to ballads, rather than the faster bebop tunes. The
> slower tempo and longer-held melody notes in ballads will be much easier for your first project.

**Significance (p. 18).** Establishes the genre/tempo constraint that makes step 1 practical for beginners.

## Range rule of thumb

> Search for a key where you can play the melody on the top 3 strings, roughly anywhere from the
>  3rd to the 9th fret.

**Significance (p. 19).** Concrete, fretboard-region rule that drives the key-change decision in step 2.

## Range failure modes

> If the arrangement is too high, it will sound like it doesn’t have enough depth.
>    On the other hand, if your arrangement is too low in the register, it will sound too dark and
>    muddy.

**Significance (p. 19).** Names both failure modes that the range rule of thumb is designed to avoid.

## Horizontal melody fingering

> This will mean you will need to play more horizontally over the fretboard, rather than staying in a
> single position.

**Significance (p. 20).** Defines the fretting-hand approach that makes top-2-string melody fingering possible.

## Don't harmonize every note

> You don’t need a chord on every melody note.

**Significance (p. 23).** Headline pedagogical rule guarding against the most common chord-melody overplaying mistake.

## Place chords on long notes

> Notice on the above example, I mainly place chords on the long-held melody notes. It sounds great
> and is easy on the hands.

**Significance (p. 23).** Operationalizes the 'don't chord every note' rule by naming where chords should land.

## Sustain workaround

> The long melody notes ring out quickly
> on guitar, so rather than trying to hold them for their full length, I place a chord voicing to emphasize
> the change of chord instead.

**Significance (p. 23).** Identifies a guitar-specific physical constraint and the chord-substitution workaround.

## Plectrum bass-then-strum

> Quick tip: When using a plectrum, pluck the bass note first, followed by a quick strum of the rest
>  of the chord (you can see me doing this in the example below) – this can be a way to negotiate
>  chord voicings with tricky string skips.

**Significance (p. 28).** Concrete right-hand technique for plectrum players to handle non-adjacent string sets.

## Right-hand technique choice

> Because chord melody playing involves a lot of – you guessed it – chords, you need a technique that
> enables you to pluck chord shapes easily.

**Significance (p. 24).** Frames why right-hand technique selection is a load-bearing decision for the whole approach.

# Chapter 2 — Module 2 - Chord Vocabulary

_Pages 32-88._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Three Harmonic Families

> A chord must belong to one of the following classifications:
>
>    Major – e.g. CMaj7, C6, CMaj9
>    Minor – e.g. Cm7, Cm9, Cm7b5
>    Dominant – e.g. C7, C9, C13, C7(b9)

**Significance (p. 33).** Foundational taxonomy O'Rourke uses to organize the whole chord dictionary.

## Minor and dominant sub-families

> Minor:
>
>    ‘m7’ sub-family – e.g. Cm7, Cm9, Cm13
>    ‘m7b5’ sub-family – e.g. Cm7b5, Cm11b5
>
> Dominant:
>
>    ‘7’ or ‘natural dominant’ sub-family – e.g. C7, C9, C13
>    ‘7alt’ or ‘altered dominant’ sub-family – e.g. C7(b9). C7(b13b9), C7(b5), C7alt

**Significance (p. 34).** Refines the 3-family taxonomy with the sub-classes that drive major-vs-minor ii-V-i voicing choice.

## Chord collection definition

> A chord collection is a group of chords that can be played over a particular chord quality or
> progression.
>
> They are useful because the sequence of chords in the collection includes every diatonic interval as
> the top note of the chords.
>
> Which means: no matter which note is in the melody line, you can harmonize it by referring to the
> chord collection.

**Significance (p. 35).** Defines O'Rourke's central organizing unit — every-interval-on-top is what makes the dictionary indexable by melody note.

## Why ii V I for collections

> The ii-V-I progression contains a chord from each harmonic family:
>
>    ii – minor
>    V – dominant
>    I – major

**Significance (p. 48).** Justifies organizing the chord vocabulary around ii-V-I as a single-progression cover of all three families.

## 2nd-string collection rationale

> As covered in module 1, when arranging chord melodies it’s helpful to have the melody mainly on the
> top 1st and 2nd strings.
>
> So far, the chord voicings we have learned in this lesson have the highest note on the 1st string.
>
> Now let’s add additional chord collections that have their highest note on the 2nd string.

**Significance (p. 57).** Names why the dictionary is duplicated into 1st-string and 2nd-string variants — to match the top-2-string melody constraint.

## Chord symbol definition

> A chord symbol is an attempt to describe the type of sound produced by a group of notes.

**Significance (p. 74).** O'Rourke's definitional framing for the symbol-as-formula approach.

## Stacking thirds

> Chords are constructed most often by stacking thirds.

**Significance (p. 75).** Core construction principle behind every voicing in the dictionary.

## Second-octave structures

> This is where chords like GMaj13 come from – from continuing to stack more and more thirds
> onto the structure, which gives us more and more colorful extensions

**Significance (p. 78).** Explains why extension numbers go past 7 and where 9/11/13 chord names originate.

## Theoretical vs playable

> On the guitar, there is a difference between the theoretically correct chord and the playable chord.
>
> Often notes can be omitted from the chord shape to get a similar enough sound, but with a much
> easier shape.

**Significance (p. 79).** Names the gap between theory and playable guitar voicings that the omittable-notes rule fills.

## Omittable notes list

> Intervals most commonly omitted include the 5th, the root (1st), the 11th, and the 9th. Note though
> that even the 3rd and the 7th can be omitted for more complex chords in certain situations as well.

**Significance (p. 79).** Specific rule for which notes can drop out of a voicing while preserving essential chord character.

## Extensions vs altered tensions

> An extension (sometimes called a ‘natural’ or ‘diatonic’ tension) is an interval of the chord which
> naturally occurs in the parent scale of the key of the progression. Extensions are included as part of
> the chord name. Examples are G9, G13, and GMaj9.
>
> Altered tensions are notes that are outside the parent scale of the key of the progression, and are
> (usually) added in parentheses after the chord name. Examples are GMaj7(#11), G7(b13#9).

**Significance (p. 80).** Defines the diatonic/altered distinction that drives chord-symbol notation throughout the book.

## Context defines chord name

> A chord’s sound is as much defined by the chords that are around it in the progression, just as much
> as the notes of the chord themselves.

**Significance (p. 84).** Conceptual rule that justifies why the same notes get relabeled and why context drives voicing choice.

# Chapter 3 — Module 3 - Let's Create a Chord Melody Arrangement (First Steps & Harmony Analysis)

_Pages 89-111._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Top-2-string melody constraint

> In order to fit our chord voicings underneath the melody notes, we need to change the fingering of
> the melody to sit on the top 2 (or 3) strings.
>
> Note: it’s ok to occasionally dip down the 3rd or even the 4th string if necessary – don’t get too rigid
> about only sticking to 2 strings!

**Significance (p. 98).** Reaffirms the top-2-string canvas rule with the explicit carve-out that prevents over-rigid fingering.

## Write down the arrangement

> Whatever method you choose, it’s important to write down your arrangement somehow. Writing gives
> you the ability to keep track of your ideas and thus refine them more easily (which can be difficult to
> do if you are arranging entirely by ear).

**Significance (p. 94).** Process rule: the arrangement must be notated (paper or software) for iterative refinement to work.

## Basic comping before voicing

> Something good to do before getting into the nitty-gritty of what voicings to use is to simply learn
> the basic chords of the song as if comping in a band.
>
> This is another way to train your ears to the chord progression and is a gentle way to dip your toes
> into applying chords to your chosen tune.

**Significance (p. 100).** Pre-step in the method: comp the song first so the ear knows the harmony before voicing selection.

## Harmonic analysis goals

> We are aiming to get 3 outcomes from this:
>
> 1. To work out the quality of each chord in the progression (major, minor, dominant).
> 2. To observe any key changes in the tune.
> 3. To see if we can spot common chord sequences in the progression, such as ii – V – Is etc.

**Significance (p. 105).** Defines the three specific outputs the analysis pass must produce before voicing assignment.

## Roman numeral conventions

> Harmonic analysis uses the following conventions:
>
>    Roman numbers are designated to indicate a chord’s function.
>    Upper case Roman numerals indicate a chord being major or dominant.
>    Lower case Roman numerals indicate a chord being minor.

**Significance (p. 105).** Notation rules that make the analysis output legible to other musicians and to the reader's own future self.

## Why interval analysis

> If we know what interval a melody note is in relation to a given chord, we will be able to find an
> appropriate voicing from our chord dictionary with that interval as the top note chord.

**Significance (p. 107).** States the bridge between melody analysis and dictionary lookup — the linchpin of the painting-by-numbers method.

## Interval relates to current chord

> The interval number we give a melody note must relate to the current chord in the
>  progression – NOT the overall key of the progression. This is a common mistake beginner
>  arrangers often make.

**Significance (p. 107).** Flags the most common student error and the correct mental model for interval labeling.

## Chord-tone vocabulary

> The interval numbers are the chord tone numbers, and could be one of the following:
>
>  1, b9, 9, b3, 3, 11, #11, 5,b13, 13, b7, 7

**Significance (p. 107).** Closed vocabulary of interval labels the dictionary is indexed on — essential for the lookup step.

## Passing-tone label

> Often in jazz melodies, the chromatic notes in a melody can be considered passing tones if they
>  are not on a strong downbeat and simply lead to a stronger chord tone, so you can use the label
>  PT for those.

**Significance (p. 107).** Defines the PT escape hatch for chromatic melody notes that don't get harmonized.

## Major scale reference rule

> To find the correct interval number, you always refer to the major scale of the current chord.

**Significance (p. 108).** Defines the reference scale used for labeling — not the chord's own scale, but always major-scale-of-the-root.

## Golden rule of interval analysis

> Always relate the interval to the chord at that point in the chord progression.

**Significance (p. 110).** O'Rourke's headline golden rule for the whole interval-analysis pass.

## Melody anticipates next chord

> Sometimes a melody note anticipates the next chord so is considered an interval of that
> chord (see the ends of bar 1, 3, 5, 7).

**Significance (p. 111).** Edge-case rule for how to handle melody notes that arrive before their harmonic context changes.

# Chapter 4 — Module 3 - Painting by Numbers and Arrangement Examples

_Pages 112-136._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Where to place chords

> This final step is a straightforward process of applying a chord to the first melody note of each bar
> and/or chord change in the lead sheet.

**Significance (p. 113).** Concrete placement rule for the painting-by-numbers method — chord on bar 1 or chord-change beat.

## Painting by numbers rule

> Choose chord voicings that have the same interval of the melody note as the highest note in the
>  chord shape.

**Significance (p. 114).** The single load-bearing voicing-selection rule that the whole method depends on.

## Adapt voicing for melody

> a voicing that can be slightly adapted to have the melody note as the highest note and still sound
> good.

**Significance (p. 114).** Carve-out from the strict rule: voicings can be tweaked rather than always matched exactly.

## Three voicing-lookup inputs

> To know which voicing to pick we need to establish:
>
> 1. The string the melody note is on
> 2. The type of chord that is in the chord progression whilst that melody note is played
> 3. The interval of the melody note in relation to that chord.

**Significance (p. 114).** Defines the three index keys for the chord-dictionary lookup.

## Transpose by sliding shape

> Easily fixed – just shift the shape down on the fretboard so that the top note matches the same fret
> where the melody note is on our score:

**Significance (p. 118).** Operational rule for adapting a dictionary voicing into the key of the tune at hand.

## Less is more

> In fact, less is more – placing chords at key places ‘here and there’ is easier on the hands and is
> usually more than enough to imply the harmony beneath the melody line. So don’t get carried away!

**Significance (p. 122).** Pedagogical rule against overharmonization, with the implied-harmony rationale.

## Playability over ambition

> “If it’s easy on the hands, the music will flow better – so most of the time making things as easy
>  as possible is musically the best option anyway”.

**Significance (p. 124).** Cited rule that justifies cutting voicings to improve playability — the key principle behind the refine step.

## Three options for hard passages

> We have some options here to make this passage more playable:
>
> 1. Cut out notes from the chord – e.g. we could cut that low B to make it easier to change to the
> next chord:

**Significance (p. 125).** Names the first of three remediation strategies for awkward voicings.

## Diads/octaves alternative

> 2. Choose a different harmonization approach – e.g. instead of block chords I could use parallel
> octaves or diads (a “chord” with only two notes). Here’s an example of diads in 3rds:

**Significance (p. 125).** Second remediation strategy and definition of diads in the method.

## Removing chord entirely

> Removing the difficult voicings completely often makes the whole passage work much better on the
> guitar – it helps my hands to relax.

**Significance (p. 126).** Third remediation strategy — author's most-used escape valve for awkward voicings.

## Tension from dominants

> The jazzy sound in arrangements usually comes from adding extensions and tensions to voicings –
> particularly to dominant chords.

**Significance (p. 127).** Rule of thumb: where the 'jazzy' quality lives and which chord type to target for adding tensions.

# Chapter 5 — Module 4 - Arranging For Solo Guitar (Techniques)

_Pages 137-199._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Illusion of solo guitar

> A good solo guitar arranger simply suggests these elements throughout the performance. Play a
> measure of walking bass, followed by a chord line, followed by unaccompanied single lines –
> alternating through these different devices gives the ‘illusion’ to the audience of a complete band.
>
> The listener’s mind will fill in the gaps.

**Significance (p. 138).** Defines the core aesthetic strategy for solo arranging — alternation, not simultaneity, sells the band illusion.

## Bass on 5th/6th strings

> The key difference between a ‘trio’ chord melody arrangement (to be played in a band) and solo
> guitar is the greater prominence of bass notes on the 5th and 6th strings.
>
> You no longer have a bass player to play with, so you need to fill that role ourselves – otherwise, the
> arrangement will sound thin and empty.

**Significance (p. 140).** Names the single load-bearing difference between trio and solo arranging in O'Rourke's framework.

## Open-string keys

> An excellent way around this is to choose a key for your arrangement that allows the use of open
> strings – especially for the bass notes.
>
> Choosing a key like A, E, or D gives you essentially another set of free fingers. Without needing to fret
> the occasional bass note, you can make things much easier technically.

**Significance (p. 142).** Specific key-selection rule for solo guitar — prefer A/E/D so open-string bass frees the fretting hand.

## Bass-upper proximity issue

> A common issue with many jazz guitar arrangements is that the bass is too close to the upper
> harmony, which results in a thin sound.

**Significance (p. 142).** Diagnostic for why solo arrangements sound thin and the spacing failure mode to avoid.

## Shell voicing definition

> These are called shell voicings – i.e. the ‘bare bones’ of what you need to define the sound of the
> chord: the root, 3rd, and 7th.

**Significance (p. 145).** Definition of the solo-guitar voicing primitive: root + 3rd + 7th, with the 5th removed.

## Drop the 5th

> You can remove the 5th from the chord and still retain the essential character of the sound.

**Significance (p. 145).** Specific rule for how shells are derived from bar chords — the 5th is the first cut.

## Avoid doubled notes

> Generally, try to avoid doubling notes in the chord – I’ve usually cut out notes from the voicing
> when doubling occurs. However, sometimes it still sounds ok so I’ve left doublings in when this is the
> case.

**Significance (p. 153).** Rule for refining solo voicings: cut doublings unless the doubling happens to sound good.

## 6 or 7 substitution

> Here are the shell voicings that are commonly used in jazz guitar arrangements (note that the 6th
> interval can also be used instead of the 7th which gives a warmer, more resolved sound):

**Significance (p. 147).** Gives the 6th-for-7th interval substitution as a tonal-color option in shells.

## Bass-notes-at-changes drill

> To get a feel for where to place shell voicings, a good exercise is to add a bass note underneath the
> melody at each chord change, like this:

**Significance (p. 157).** Concrete intermediate drill in the solo arranging workflow before voicings get added.

## Arpeggiation definition

> Arpeggiation is when you pluck the notes of a chord one at a time, instead of a block of notes
>   played simultaneously.

**Significance (p. 169).** Definitional content for one of the four core solo-guitar devices in this chapter.

## Octaves definition

> Octaves are a case of doubling the melody one octave down.

**Significance (p. 174).** Definitional content for the Wes-Montgomery-style octave device.

## Diads definition

> Diads are when you harmonize a line in a parallel interval, usually 3rds or 6ths.

**Significance (p. 180).** Definitional content for the Barney-Kessel-style diad device.

## Octave muting technique

> When playing the octaves, mute the unwanted open string between the octave notes by gently
> touching the string with your first finger.

**Significance (p. 179).** Specific left-hand technique that makes octave-playing actually workable.

## Walking bass four-step build

> To complete your walking bassline, you just need to add two more notes in between the root note of
> beat 1, and the approach note of beat 4.
>
> These can be any of the following:
>
>    Diatonic notes from the scale of the given key.
>    Chromatic notes, or
>    Arpeggios.

**Significance (p. 185).** Specific recipe for filling beats 2-3 in O'Rourke's walking-bass construction method.

## Chromatic approach chord

> In this case, you can use a voicing on the note being approached, then play the same voicing one fret
> away for the approach note in the melody:

**Significance (p. 197).** Concrete passing-chord rule for chromatic approach notes — parallel voicing one fret away.

# Chapter 6 — Module 4 - Solo Guitar Arrangement Examples

_Pages 200-212._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Learn the arrangements hands-on

> Observe the devices used in the arrangements as marked, and it would be very beneficial to actually
> learn these arrangements – getting your ‘hands dirty’ with some examples is one of the best ways to
> internalize the techniques that are being used, which you can then apply to your own arrangements.

**Significance (p. 200).** States O'Rourke's core pedagogical instruction for this module: internalize technique through hands-on study of complete arrangements, not abstract reading.

## Arpeggiation fills ballad space

> This rendition is a good example of using arpeggiation to fill in the long spaces between each melody
> note in this slow ballad.

**Significance (p. 201).** Names the load-bearing arranging device for slow ballads in O'Rourke's method: arpeggiation as a melody-space filler.

## Diads support the melody

> Diads are also used frequently to provide support to the melody line.

**Significance (p. 201).** Identifies diads as a primary melody-support device in O'Rourke's chord-melody toolkit.

## Up-tempo devices stack

> You can see the diads, octaves, and passing chords being a feature in this rendition.

**Significance (p. 206).** Catalogues the three load-bearing devices O'Rourke uses to drive an up-tempo arrangement: diads, octaves, and passing chords.

## Manipulate melody rhythm for fingering

> I needed to manipulate the rhythm of the melody in places to facilitate easier jumps across the
> fretboard.

**Significance (p. 206).** Gives the explicit rule that rhythmic adjustment of the melody is acceptable when fretboard jumps would otherwise be impractical.

# Chapter 7 — Module 5 - More Advanced Arranging Techniques

_Pages 213-263._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Substitution defined

> A substitution simply means replacing one chord with another.

**Significance (p. 213).** Provides the module's foundational definition that all later substitution techniques build on.

## Tritone substitution defined

> Tritone substitution is the process of substituting one chord for another a tritone away.

**Significance (p. 214).** Defines the most useful harmonic substitution in jazz per O'Rourke.

## Guide tones explain tritone sub

> C7 has E as the 3rd and Bb as the 7th.
>
> F#7 has E as the 7th and A# (which is the same as Bb) as the 3rd.
>
> The guide tones have essentially swapped places between these two chords.

**Significance (p. 216).** Gives the theoretical justification for tritone substitution via shared 3rd/7th guide tones.

## Common-tones enable substitution

> Because the two chords have those 3rd and 7th as common tones, they can be substituted for
>  one another.

**Significance (p. 216).** States the underlying principle (shared guide tones) that makes tritone substitution work.

## Tritone Substitution Rule

> Tritone Substitution Rule - You can replace any chord with another whose root is a tritone
>  away from the root of the original chord.

**Significance (p. 220).** Codifies the canonical tritone substitution rule in O'Rourke's own pedagogical formulation.

## Don't overuse substitutions

> Keeping things simple most of the time and just adding a substitution occasionally to brighten up the
> arrangement is the approach I find works best – a good arrangement is all about subtlety!

**Significance (p. 222).** Provides the taste/restraint rule that governs all of O'Rourke's substitution methods.

## Relative key definition

> G major and E minor are called relative key signatures to each other because they share the
>  same key signature.

**Significance (p. 223).** Defines the relative-key relationship that underpins the I-to-vi substitution rule.

## I to vi substitution rule

> Rule: you can replace the I chord of a key with the vi chord of the key.

**Significance (p. 224).** Codifies the relative-substitution rule for major-to-minor reharmonization.

## I to iii substitution rule

> Rule: you can substitute a major chord for chord iii of its key.

**Significance (p. 225).** Gives the second major-to-minor substitution rule, completing the major-to-minor pair.

## Chord addition defined

> This process is referred to as chord addition – adding extra chords to the harmony.

**Significance (p. 228).** Names the technique of inserting substitute chords as additions to the original harmony.

## Minor conversion concept

> Pat Martino was a great proponent of what’s known as the "minor conversion concept" - a theory
>  that any chord you see in a progression could be substituted for a minor chord instead.

**Significance (p. 229).** Cites the Pat Martino concept that licenses minor-for-anything substitution in this method.

## Minor for Dominant Substitution Rule

> You can use a minor 7 chord from the 5th of any dominant chord (which is like using the iim7 that
>  belongs to the dominant chord).
>
>  For example: an F7 chord’s 5th interval is C. So you could play Cm7 over F7 to get an interesting
>  sound.

**Significance (p. 230).** Codifies the minor-for-dominant substitution as a concrete rule with worked example.

## Back cycling root movement

> When one cycles anti-clockwise (backward) through the cycle of 5ths, we get a sequence of root
> tones that are separated by the interval of a 4th.
>
> This cycle of root notes a 4th apart is the most common root movement in Western music.

**Significance (p. 237).** Defines back cycling and grounds it in the most common root movement in the genre.

## Back cycling substitution rule

> Back cycling substitution rule - you can add chords to the chord progression of a song based
>  on the ‘back cycle’, i.e. the circle of fourths.

**Significance (p. 243).** Codifies the back-cycling reharmonization rule in canonical form.

## Melodic common tone substitution rule

> Melodic common tone substitution rule: substitute any chord for another chord, as long as
>  the new chord has a note in common with it that you use as the melody note on top.

**Significance (p. 247).** Codifies O'Rourke's fifth substitution approach, the one that produces modern Rosenwinkel/Kreisberg-style colors.

## Practice with metronome on 2 and 4

> Have the metronome click on every beat.
>    Then try the metronome clicking on beats 1 and 3 only.
>    Then have a go with the metronome on beats 2 and 4 only.
>
> The final version is most desirable for jazz as it helps give the music more of a swing.

**Significance (p. 252).** Gives the concrete metronome progression and identifies the 2-and-4 click as the jazz-preferred form.

## Practice 1-2 bars per session

> Instead, methodically work through just 1 or 2 bars per session. Spend time slowly practicing the
> fingering and carefully checking the notes are accurate and that everything is moving comfortably
> under your fingers.

**Significance (p. 253).** Specifies the per-session learning quantum O'Rourke prescribes for mastering an arrangement.

# Chapter 8 — Appendix 1 - Chord Dictionaries

_Pages 264-285._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Chord melody dictionary purpose

> This page features a handy reference of all the chord voicings that comprise the chord dictionary
> covered in Module 3.

**Significance (p. 265).** States the role of the chord-melody dictionary as a consolidated reference for the Module 3 voicings.

## Major II V I string set 4321

> MAJOR II V I CHORDS – STRING SET 4321

**Significance (p. 266).** Names a load-bearing voicing family in O'Rourke's method: major ii V I voicings on the top four strings.

## Major II V I string set 5432

> MAJOR II V I CHORDS – STRING SET 5432

**Significance (p. 269).** Names the second canonical string set for major ii V I voicings, completing the pair.

## Minor II V I string set 4321

> MINOR II V I CHORDS – STRING SET 4321

**Significance (p. 272).** Names the minor-key counterpart string set, including the b5 and b9 alterations characteristic of minor ii V i.

## Minor II V I string set 5432

> MINOR II V I CHORDS – STRING SET 5432

**Significance (p. 275).** Names the second minor-key string set, completing the four-way taxonomy of ii V i voicings in this method.

## Tensions and extensions on dominants

> TENSIONS & EXTENSIONS ON DOMINANT CHORDS

**Significance (p. 278).** Heads the dictionary section covering altered/extended dominants, a load-bearing color resource for the method.

## Solo guitar shell voicings

> Here’s an overview of useful solo guitar voicings as featured in module 4.

**Significance (p. 280).** Frames the second dictionary section as the canonical solo-guitar voicing reference for Module 4.

## Shell voicings root on 6th

> SHELL VOICINGS
>
> Root on the 6th String

**Significance (p. 280).** Names the 6th-string-root shell-voicing family, the structural backbone of O'Rourke's solo-guitar comping.

## Shell voicing extensions catalog

> SHELL VOICING EXTENSIONS

**Significance (p. 281).** Heads the catalog of extensions added on top of shells, the load-bearing harmonic vocabulary for solo arranging.

# Chapter 9 — Appendix 2 - Fingerstyle Training

_Pages 286-317._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Fingerstyle plays multiple voices

> This technique allows you to easily play multiple voices at once on one guitar – enabling you to create
> complex chord melody arrangements and chord solos.

**Significance (p. 286).** Justifies fingerstyle as the enabling technique for the entire chord-melody method.

## PIMAC finger naming

> p = thumb (“pulgar” in Spanish)
>    i = index (“indice” in Spanish)
>    m = middle (“medio” in Spanish)
>    a = ring finger (“anular” in Spanish)
>    c = pinky finger (“chiquito” in Spanish)

**Significance (p. 287).** Defines the standard finger-naming convention used throughout the appendix and in all picking-pattern markings.

## Early hand position matters

> It’s very important for fingerstyle that, right from the start, you adopt a hand position that
> promotes agility and freedom of movement.

**Significance (p. 287).** States the foundational ergonomic rule that governs every fingerstyle exercise in the appendix.

## Tips for Correct Fingerstyle Hand Placement

> Keep the wrist naturally curved in your plucking hand – avoid flatting or overarching the wrist too
>    much.
>    Keep the wrist in alignment with the arm, avoid kinking the wrist at an angle (it’s ok to strike the
>    strings slightly diagonally).
>    Keep your hand relaxed as possible, take frequent breaks while you are still learning these
>    techniques.
>    Fingernails can be helpful for getting more depth and tone of the sound, but is entirely optional
>    (especially on electric guitar).

**Significance (p. 288).** Catalogues the four concrete hand-position rules that constitute O'Rourke's fingerstyle posture method.

## Rest stroke vs free stroke defined

> “Rest stroke” – when you play a string and then rest your finger on the string behind the one
>    you plucked at the end of the motion.
>
>    “Free stroke” – when you pluck the string and let your finger fly “freely” above the other strings
>    as you end the motion.

**Significance (p. 289).** Defines the two foundational plucking strokes that all subsequent exercises use.

## Prefer free stroke for jazz

> Although rest stroke is a good first stroke to learn to gain security with your plucking, at this point I
> would recommend focusing on free stroke as your main picking technique.
>
> In general, free stroke is a more much more useful for jazz guitar than rest stroke.

**Significance (p. 293).** Gives the explicit preference rule between the two strokes for the jazz-guitar use case.

## Thumb motion mechanics

> The motion should come from the lowest “3rd” joint of the thumb, with a small circular motion as you
> pluck – similar to the motion of a paddle on a rowing boat.

**Significance (p. 294).** Specifies the exact mechanical motion of the thumb, a load-bearing technical instruction.

## Thumb role in fingerstyle jazz

> For a typical fingerstyle jazz guitarist, the thumb is generally reserved for playing bass notes in
> chords or to play basslines alongside melodies on the higher strings.

**Significance (p. 294).** Defines the conventional functional role of the thumb in this method.

## Wrist angle for multi-string plucks

> Ensure that your wrist is rolled slightly more towards the headstock to allow more space between the
> index finger and thumb. If your hand position is incorrect, these two fingers will obstruct each other’s
> motion when those fingers pluck notes together.

**Significance (p. 298).** Gives the specific wrist-angle correction needed to make multi-string plucking ergonomically work.

## Two-finger pluck combinations

> The main ways to pluck adjacent strings is with the p and i fingers, or i and m fingers.

**Significance (p. 298).** Codifies the two canonical two-finger plucking pairs for adjacent-string work.

## Three-note pluck combinations

> For 3 notes at once, it’s preferable to use p i m, or i m a, so it’s worth practicing both these
>  combinations.

**Significance (p. 298).** Codifies the two canonical three-finger plucking groupings used in this method.

## Four-finger maximum, skip pinky

> I rarely use the little finger (c) on the right hand with fingerstyle technique – I tend to stick to 4-note
> voicings as a maximum which means you will only need to use your four strongest fingers – p i m a.

**Significance (p. 298).** States the explicit voicing-size cap (four notes) and the rule to omit the pinky from the picking hand.

## Sound multi-string notes simultaneously

> Regardless of which fingers you use, ensure that both fingers sound the notes simultaneously.

**Significance (p. 298).** Gives the load-bearing rule that distinguishes a clean chord pluck from an arpeggiation.
